comment.body:
Hi Alma👸🏼,I'm a bit confused by this, so l'll try to explain my understanding:
We have that very large tall at 5700, and the speed curve is increasing. This seems counterintuitive because if we move upward with the spot, gamma decreases, so we would expect a negative speed— since the derivative of a decreasing curve is negative.
Essentially, we find ourselves in local negative speed as soon as the curve drops from that gamma peak, but within a broader environment of positive speed.
Am I missing the counterintuitive part? 
that “counterintuitive” fucked me over.
comment.raw_body:

Essentially, we find ourselves in local negative speed as soon as the curve drops from that gamma peak, but within a broader environment of positive speed.
Am I missing the counterintuitive part? 
that “counterintuitive” fucked me over.

comment.body:
bcs if you look at the CURVATURE ot the gamma there, you can see that it is going up as we move up on spot, so it is negative speed, but the CURVATURE itself is decreasing. 
so speed goes up/longer

comment.body:
GS: The most unusual flow on our radar is the steady buying of single stocks by retail investors. The significant buying began on January 29th, suggesting positive sentiment coming out of the FOMC decision that afternoon. Buying slowed modestly around the 12-Feb CPI, but then resumed in earnest on 14-Feb. Buying was larger in Nasdaq 100 stocks, but was also apparent in S&P 500 stocks.
Single stock options volumes are driven by call buying among retail investors and hedge funds: Options volumes among single stocks are roughly flat over the past quarter at an elevated level and have likely played a role in the outperformance of stocks that are commonly associated with retail investors. As we approach the earnings reports of key AI stocks over the next couple of weeks, we will be closely watching how these volumes trend. A sustained decline in volumes would be a sign of waning enthusiasm and may embolden hedge funds to increase their short positions.

Single stock options volumes are driven by call buying among retail investors and hedge funds: Options volumes among single stocks are roughly flat over the past quarter at an elevated level and have likely played a role in the outperformance of stocks that are commonly associated with retail investors. As we approach the earnings reports of key AI stocks over the next couple of weeks, we will be closely watching how these volumes trend. A sustained decline in volumes would be a sign of waning enthusiasm and may embolden hedge funds to increase their short positions.

comment.body:
Here is my understanding of the logic. Timespan is from now to Thursday.

1. 22dP/22dC going up means either 22dP going up or 22dC going down or both.
2. 22dP/ATM going down/stagnating means 22dP going down (Really?)
3. "22dC going down" is dominanting.


But I'm not sure about the "2. 22dP/ATM going down/stagnating means 22dP going down".
ATM itself is going up and 22dp/ATM is going down/stagnating. It could also be possible that 22dP is going up but ATM is going up more, isn't it?

Alma, this is a relatively simple question, but how accurate is the statement that "the delta of an option at any given time is the rough approximation of how likely it is to expire ITM?" Thank you
